1: The tabernacle furnishings that Hebrews 9:1-5 inventories correspond to the Exodus descriptions: the lampstand (Exodus 25:31-40; Numbers 8:1-4), the table of showbread (Exodus 25:23-30; Leviticus 24:5-9), the golden altar of incense (Exodus 30:1-10; placed either in front of the curtain [Exodus 30:6] or associated with the inner sanctuary in Hebrews' account), the Ark of the Covenant with its gold jar of manna (Exodus 16:33-34), Aaron's budded staff (Numbers 17:10), the stone tablets (Exodus 25:21; Deuteronomy 10:1-5), and the cherubim of glory overshadowing the mercy seat (Exodus 25:17-22). The Mishnah tractate Middot and Yoma describe the Second Temple's actual arrangement, which differed somewhat from the desert tabernacle. Hebrews appears to describe the Mosaic tabernacle rather than the Herodian temple.
7: The Day of Atonement (Yom Kippur) ritual is described in detail in Leviticus 16 and elaborated extensively in the Mishnah tractate Yoma. The elaborate preparations: the high priest separated from his family and household seven days before; he immersed in the mikveh (ritual bath) five times on the day itself; he offered the bull for himself, then selected one of two goats for the LORD (the other becoming the scapegoat for Azazel); he entered the Most Holy Place three times — with the incense pan, then with the bull's blood, then with the goat's blood — sprinkling seven times on the mercy seat and seven times in front of it. The elaborate preparation for a single annual entry underscored the difficulty of access to the divine presence that Hebrews argues Christ's death permanently resolves.
11: The Hebrews statement that Christ is "a high priest of the good things that have come" (or "that are coming," depending on the manuscript) places him in the context of the eschatological good that Jewish apocalyptic anticipated. 4 Ezra 7:26-44 describes the revelation of the age to come with its messianic kingdom and final judgment. 2 Baruch 29:3-8 describes the eschatological abundance (manna from heaven again, wine from vines) that will accompany the Messiah's arrival. The "greater and more perfect tent not made with hands" uses cheiropoiētos (made by human hands) — a term the Hebrew prophets used to describe idols (Isaiah 2:18; 10:11) and that Acts 7:48 uses to argue that God does not dwell in a human-made temple. The heavenly sanctuary transcends the category of human religious construction.
13: The red heifer ritual (Num 19) was among the most puzzling of the Torah's purity laws — even King Solomon, tradition said, could not explain it (Numbers Rabbah Hebrews 19:3; Pesikta de-Rav Kahana 4.2). The ashes of the red heifer, mixed with water, purified those who had become unclean through contact with a corpse. The ritual required that the officiating priest himself became unclean in the process of purifying others — a paradox that the Mishnah (Parah) acknowledges without resolving. The rabbis treated it as a chok (divine decree beyond human understanding). Hebrews reads the ritual's very paradoxicality as part of its typological function: the shadow of purification through the death of the unblemished animal, which itself defiled the purifier, points forward to the blood of Christ that purifies without defiling its source.
2: The lampstand (Menorah) in the first room of the tabernacle was a seven-branched golden lamp described in Exodus 25:31-40. In Second Temple practice, the Menorah burned continuously and became one of the most recognized symbols of Jewish identity — Josephus (Jewish War 7.148) describes its image on the Arch of Titus in Rome, where it was displayed as war-spoil after Jerusalem's fall. The showbread (lechem ha-panim, bread of the presence) consisted of twelve loaves arranged on the golden table, replaced weekly on the Sabbath (Leviticus 24:5-9); the replaced loaves were eaten by the priests. These furnishings in the Holy Place (accessible to priests) contrasted with the absolute privacy of the Most Holy Place — the gradient of access to the divine presence was built into the sanctuary's physical architecture.
3: The second curtain (katapetasma) that separated the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place was the barrier described in Exodus 26:31-33 — made of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn and fine twisted linen, embroidered with cherubim. The Mishnah (Shekalim 8:4-5) records that the second Temple had two curtains, one cubit apart, with the priests entering from the south side and exiting from the north to avoid turning their backs to the sanctuary. The curtain's tearing at Jesus's crucifixion (Matthew 27:51; Mark 15:38; Luke 23:45) was understood in the synoptic tradition as a sign of opened divine access — the barrier removed. Hebrews develops this symbolism by identifying the curtain with Christ's flesh (10:20) — the barrier that was the locus of access when torn.
4: The ark of the covenant (aron ha-berit) was the most sacred object in Israelite religion — the throne-footstool of the divine presence (1 Chr 28:2; Psalms 132:7-8), the container of the Torah (Deuteronomy 10:5), and the location where God spoke to Moses (Exodus 25:22). The first Temple's Most Holy Place contained the ark; the Second Temple had no ark — it had been lost (presumably during the Babylonian conquest), and the Second Temple's Most Holy Place was empty. The Mishnah (Yoma 5:2) notes that the high priest sprinkled blood in the place "where the ark used to be." By Hebrews' time, the Second Temple audience knew the Most Holy Place was empty of the ark — making the argument about Christ's entry into the true heavenly sanctuary even more pointed: the earthly Most Holy Place was already an empty symbol.
5: The cherubim above the ark (Ex 25:17-22) were understood as the guardians of the divine throne-presence, whose wings formed the seat on which the invisible divine glory rested. The divine title "the LORD of hosts who is enthroned above the cherubim" (1 Sam 4:4; 2 Sam 6:2; Isaiah 37:16) associated the cherubim specifically with divine sovereignty and holy-war power. In Second Temple angelology, cherubim were identified as the highest order of angelic beings — 1 Enoch and the Qumran Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice describe the cherubim's role in the heavenly throne-room liturgy. The mercy seat (kapporet) where the blood was sprinkled was therefore not merely a ritual object but the earthly focal point of the divine throne — the place where heaven and earth met, and where the blood of atonement was presented to God.
6: The regular priestly ministry in the outer room (the Holy Place) was the daily routine of Temple service: the lighting of the lamps, the burning of incense morning and evening, and the weekly replacement of the showbread. The Mishnah tractate Tamid describes the daily Temple service in detail: the daily whole-burnt offering (tamid) at dawn and dusk, the incense offering twice daily, the priestly divisions (mishmarot) that rotated service weekly. Twenty-four priestly divisions (1 Chronicles 24:7-19) took turns serving in the Temple throughout the year. This regular, repeated service was in many ways the heartbeat of Jewish religious life — the daily rhythm by which Israel maintained its relationship with God through the mediating priestly caste.
8: The Holy Spirit's pedagogical communication through the tabernacle structure reflects Hebrews' understanding that the entire Mosaic cult was divinely designed to teach through its limitations. The rabbinic principle that the Torah's commands have both surface and deeper meanings (peshat and derash) enabled interpreters to find theological significance in ritual details. Philo (On the Life of Moses 2.71-108) gave elaborate allegorical explanations for every element of the tabernacle. The specific lesson Hebrews draws — that the curtain-barrier taught that the way to God was not yet open — turns the tabernacle's most prominent limitation into its deepest teaching: the architecture itself was a visual sermon about the inadequacy of the old covenant access to God.
9: The tabernacle as a "parable for the present time" (parabolē eis ton kairon ton enestēkota) reflects the typological hermeneutic that Hebrews shares with Jewish midrashic interpretation, though differently applied. The Qumran pesher method read ancient texts as direct address to the community's present situation; rabbinic midrash found contemporary lessons in every Torah detail. Hebrews reads the tabernacle's structure as an extended parable pointing beyond itself to the Christological fulfillment. The specific point — that the gifts and sacrifices could not perfect the worshipper "according to conscience" — addresses the inner-life dimension that the external ritual system acknowledged it could not reach.
10: "Food and drink and various ceremonial washings" describes the external purity regulations that governed access to the sanctuary and participation in communal meals. Leviticus 11-15 covers dietary laws, impurity from bodily discharges, and the purification rituals required after various forms of impurity. The Qumran community intensified these purity requirements considerably, requiring ritual immersion before community meals and maintaining strict dietary separation from outsiders. The Pharisees developed elaborate applications of the purity laws for everyday life (the "tradition of the elders" that Jesus debated in Mark 7). Hebrews characterizes these laws as "external regulations applying until the time of the new order" — acknowledging their temporary legitimacy while asserting that the time of the new order (diorthōsis) has arrived.
12: The Yom Kippur blood-ritual that Hebrews compares with Christ's entrance involved the blood of two animals: a bull for the high priest and his household, and a goat for the people (Leviticus 16:11-15). The high priest entered the Most Holy Place three times: once with the incense pan, once with the bull's blood, once with the goat's blood. Each time, he sprinkled blood on the mercy seat and in front of it. The blood of goats and calves enabled this annual ritual access but required annual repetition — the previous year's sacrifice did not carry over. Hebrews reads this structural limitation as pointing beyond itself: the annual repetition implied incompleteness, and the coming of a sufficient sacrifice would make repetition unnecessary.
14: "Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself unblemished to God" draws on the Day of Atonement requirement that the sacrificial animals be physically unblemished (tamim — Leviticus 22:19-25). The Mishnah (Parah 1-2) specifies in detail the qualifications for sacrificial animals and the disqualifications for blemish. The high priest's own physical inspection of animals before offering was part of Temple practice. The claim that Christ was "unblemished" uses this sacrificial vocabulary to position his self-offering as meeting the highest possible standard of sacrifice — not despite his humanity but in and through it, verified by the "eternal Spirit" rather than a priestly inspection.
15: The concept of covenant mediation had a rich background in Jewish theology. Moses was the mediator (mesitēs) of the Sinai covenant — standing between God and Israel at Sinai when the people feared direct approach (Exodus 20:18-21; Deuteronomy 5:5; Galatians 3:19). The high priest on Yom Kippur mediated between the sinful people and the holy God — his entrance into the Most Holy Place was the annual mediation event on which Israel's covenant standing depended. Angels were sometimes described as mediators between the divine and human realms (Galatians 3:19; Hebrews 2:2). Hebrews' claim that Jesus is the "mediator of a new covenant" positions him in this tradition while exceeding it: not merely mediating a covenant but constituting it through his death.
16: The Greek word diathēkē covered both "covenant" (the Septuagintal usage translating Hebrew berit) and "last will and testament" (the ordinary Greek legal usage). This double meaning, which Hebrews exploits in Hebrews 9:16-17, was a feature of the Greek legal environment rather than the Hebrew original. In the Greco-Roman world, a diathēkē (will) was a legal document specifying the distribution of an estate; it became binding only at the testator's death (the lex Julia de legatis of the Roman empire, and Greek equivalent practices). The metaphor works because the new covenant's promised inheritance (the forgiveness, the Spirit, the eschatological rest) could not be distributed until the covenant-maker's death had legally activated the covenant-will.
17: The legal maxim "a will is in force only when somebody has died; it never takes effect while the one who made it is living" reflects standard Hellenistic testamentary law. Papyri from Roman Egypt document wills and their conditions; the requirement that the testator be confirmed dead before distribution could begin was universally understood. In the Greco-Roman context of the letter's audience, this legal principle was as familiar as the equivalent modern legal procedure. Hebrews' application of this principle to the new covenant is a context-specific argument: for readers formed in Hellenistic legal culture, the will-analogy provided an immediately intelligible explanation of why the Messiah's death was not a problem for the covenant but its necessary precondition.
18: The Exodus 24:3-8 blood-inauguration of the Sinai covenant was a foundational narrative for Jewish understanding of covenant-making through blood. The ceremony (Moses reading the Book of the Covenant, the people's response, the sacrifice, and the blood-sprinkling on the altar and the people) established the pattern: covenants were sealed with blood that bound both parties. The phrase "the blood of the covenant" (Exodus 24:8) became a technical covenantal formula — quoted directly by Jesus at the Last Supper (Matthew 26:28; Mark 14:24) to frame his death as a covenant-inauguration event. For a Jewish audience that understood Exodus 24 as the template for covenant-making, the application of covenant-blood language to Jesus's death was immediately intelligible as a claim that Jesus's death inaugurated the new covenant Jeremiah promised.
19: The blood-sprinkling ceremony described in Exodus 24:6-8 involved hyssop and scarlet wool (also used in the red heifer ritual, Numbers 19:6) mixed with water — a composite purification medium. The Mishnah and later rabbinic texts preserve extensive discussion of the precise implements and quantities used. The addition of "calves and goats" to Hebrews' account (not in the Exodus text) may reflect liturgical expansion of the ceremony in tradition, or harmonization with the Yom Kippur blood-ritual. The sprinkling of "the scroll and all the people" emphasized that both the written law and the human community were encompassed by the covenant-blood — the covenant applied to both the textual document and the embodied people.
20: "This is the blood of the covenant, which God has commanded you to keep" (Exodus 24:8) is the formula that Jesus reuses at the Last Supper. The liturgical resonance between the Sinai blood-of-the-covenant and the eucharistic words would be unmistakable to a community that celebrated the Lord's Supper regularly. The Passover Haggadah's retelling of the Exodus events, which the synagogue and Jewish-Christian communities used, kept the Exodus covenant-making in continuous liturgical memory. The Last Supper's explicit connection of Jesus's blood to "the blood of the covenant" positioned the Christian meal in direct continuity with and supersession of the Sinai covenant-inauguration.
21: Moses's sprinkling of the tabernacle and its furnishings with blood (referenced here but not detailed in Exodus 40's account of the tabernacle's erection) reflects a priestly tradition about the comprehensive blood-consecration of sacred objects. Leviticus 8:10-11 describes Moses anointing the tabernacle with oil; later tradition may have incorporated blood-sprinkling into the comprehensive consecration. The point Hebrews makes is that the entire Mosaic system — from the covenant document to the people to the sanctuary and its furnishings — was consecrated through blood. This universal blood-consecration of the old order corresponds to the universal scope of Christ's blood-offering, which consecrates not just the earthly copies but the heavenly originals themselves (v23).
22: "Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness" reflects the theological logic underlying the entire Levitical sacrificial system. Leviticus 17:11 states the principle: "the life of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it for you on the altar to make atonement for your souls, for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life." The life-in-blood principle undergirded the substitutionary logic of sacrifice: the animal's life (blood) substituted for the worshipper's life, absorbing the death-consequence of sin. Philo (On Special Laws 1.198-199) reflects on this principle philosophically. The practical scope of blood-requirement in Leviticus was extensive — purification, ordination, guilt-offerings, sin-offerings all required blood — though Hebrews' "almost everything" (schedon) acknowledges the exceptions (fire-purification, water-purification, oil-anointing).
23: The requirement that the heavenly things themselves be purified with "better sacrifices" (plural) addresses the apparent paradox that the heavenly originals need purification. Jewish apocalyptic thought acknowledged that heavenly things could be defiled by human sin: 1 Enoch Hebrews 9:1-11 describes the archangels presenting the cry of human suffering to God; the heavenly court was understood to be affected by earthly events. The Qumran Rule of the Community (1QS Hebrews 3:4-9) describes the Spirit of Truth purifying the inner person; purification was a concept that transcended the merely physical. The "better sacrifices" (plural, corresponding to the multiple Levitical purification sacrifices) refers to the single sufficient sacrifice of Christ viewed from multiple angles of its comprehensive effect.
24: Christ's entry into "heaven itself, to appear in the presence of God on our behalf" positions him as the permanent Day-of-Atonement high priest. The Yom Kippur structure created one annual moment when the high priest stood in the divine presence as Israel's representative — the rest of the year, Israel approached God only through intermediaries at the outer altar. The priestly blessing (Numbers 6:22-27), the daily incense, and the showbread maintained continuous symbolic presence, but the Yom Kippur entry was the year's climactic moment of actual representative presence. Hebrews claims that the risen Christ maintains this representative presence permanently — not one day per year but continuously, "now to appear in the presence of God on our behalf."
25: The contrast between annual entry with "blood that is not his own" and Christ's once-for-all entry with his own blood addresses the structural problem of Yom Kippur: the high priest brought animal blood as a substitute for his own life. The prohibition on the high priest bringing his own blood was never a theological question in Levitical practice — the priest was not the sacrifice. The entire design of the Levitical system separated the sacrificer from the sacrifice. Christ's self-offering collapse this separation: the priest becomes the sacrifice. This convergence of priest and victim in one person had no precedent in the Levitical system, making it structurally impossible to accommodate Christ's priesthood within that system.
26: "At the culmination of the ages" (epi synteleia tōn aiōnōn) uses an apocalyptic technical phrase for the end-point of the current age before the new age begins. 4 Ezra 7:113 uses similar language for the eschatological judgment; the Dead Sea Scrolls (1QpHab 7:2; 1QS 4:25) use "the end of times" and "the appointed end" for the climactic moment toward which the whole of history moves. Hebrews places Jesus's death at exactly this eschatological hinge-point: not just any moment in history but the defining event that marks the transition from the old to the new. The claim that the Messiah appeared "once for all" at this moment is both a historical assertion (it happened at a specific time) and an eschatological claim (that time was the decisive turn of the ages).
27: "People are destined to die once, and after that to face judgment" states what was a near-universal conviction in Jewish eschatology, though with important debates about its details. The Pharisees affirmed bodily resurrection and post-mortem judgment (Josephus, Jewish War 2.163; Acts 23:6-8); the Sadducees denied resurrection (Matthew 22:23; Josephus, Jewish War 2.165). Wisdom of Solomon 3-5 describes the post-mortem vindication of the righteous who suffered and the condemnation of the wicked who prospered. 4 Ezra 7:32-44 presents an elaborate judgment sequence after death. The aphorism here is not a theological argument but a shared cultural assumption — the one-death / one-judgment structure of human existence is the pattern against which Christ's once-for-all sacrifice is analogized in v28.
28: "Christ was sacrificed once to take away the sins of many" echoes Isaiah 53:12 ("he bore the sin of many") from the Servant Song — one of the most debated passages in Second Temple Jewish interpretation. The Targum Jonathan interpreted Isaiah 53 of the nation of Israel rather than an individual servant; other interpretations identified the servant with Moses, or with a martyred righteous figure. The early Christian reading that applied Isaiah 53 to Jesus's death was therefore a specific interpretation in a contested exegetical tradition. The echo of "the many" (pollōn) is deliberate: it positions Christ's death as the fulfillment of the Servant's vicarious suffering, resolving the interpretive question of who the Servant was by pointing to the historical event of the crucifixion. "He will appear a second time" introduces the parousia expectation that concludes the Yom Kippur typology: after the high priest's entry into the Most Holy Place, the congregation waited anxiously for his exit — his reappearance was the signal that atonement had been accepted.
